data.title: Western Alliance: Don't Worry About First Brands
data.description:
Shares of Western Alliance Bancorporation (NYSE:WAL) dropped off more than 4% on Wednesday after reports circulated that the regional bank was financially exposed to the recent collapse of auto-parts marker First Brands Group. Western Alliance is said to have exposure to a portfolio of receivables that are linked to the company, causing selling pressure. I believe that investor concerns about this exposure are overblown given recent statements made by investment bank Jefferies, and the drop may constitute a buying opportunity for investors that have a favorable long-term view on the bank.

Previous rating

I originally bought Western Alliance Bancorporation as a contrarian bet during the regional banking meltdown after the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank in 2023. Most recently, after the lender reported solid second quarter earnings amid strong momentum in net interest income, I rated shares of Western Alliance a Buy: Solid Growth, Strong NII Gives Upside Potential. I believe current concerns about the bank's exposure to First Brands Group's receivables are exaggerated and the fallout, even in the worst-case scenario, is most likely contained.

First Brands Group situation

First Brands Group is an auto-parts supplier that filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection last month. Regional bank Western Alliance is said to have financial exposure to this company via a leveraged credit facility with Point Bonita Capital, which has renewed attention lately.

Point Bonita Capital is a trade finance platform that is managed by a division of Leucadia Asset Management which itself is part of investment bank Jefferies. Companies like First Brands Group can sell their receivables to third-party investors (a practice called "factoring") in order to shorten their cash conversion cycles and improve their liquidity positions.

Receivables that are owed by First Brands have been sold to third-party funds at a discount in exchange for immediate payment. Now that First Brands Group has fallen into bankruptcy, investors are concerned that a potential non-payment of receivables by the firm’s counterparties may negatively affect Western Alliances’ investment in the credit facility.

Point Bonita Capital’s portfolio, according to a recent disclosure by investment bank Jefferies, has about $715 million invested in receivables that are to be paid by companies like Walmart (WMT) and AutoZone (AZO):

Point Bonita Capital, a division of Leucadia Asset Management (“LAM”), manages on behalf of third-party institutional and other investors an approximately $3 billion portfolio of trade-finance assets, which is supported by total invested equity of $1.9 billion, of which $113 million, or 5.9%, is owned by LAM… Point Bonita portfolio has approximately $715 million invested in receivables.

Further, Jefferies provided further clarity on the situation on Wednesday (via Seeking Alpha):

Presently, we have no reason to believe the events from the First Brands bankruptcy create a material risk of loss in connection with the credit at this time. LAM is independently pursuing their own rights and remedies under their governing credit documents separately and apart from their obligations to Western Alliance

The exact economic exposure of Western Alliance to the receivables portfolio mentioned above is not specified, but it is unlikely that the current problems are going to have a significant impact on the bank’s earnings, in my opinion.

Even if we were to assume that the full $715M investment was provided by Western Alliance (which is highly unlikely), this amount represents only about 3.3% of the regional bank’s total securities and total cash position, and approximately 0.8% of its total assets. In other words, even in the extremely unlikely case that Western Alliance provided 100% of the investment in the leveraged credit facility with Point Bonita Capital, the bank’s operations would likely continue as normal and a potential earnings impact would not be very significant.

Further, any losses sustained here would likely be non-recurring only. In my opinion, the recent news about First Brands Group is more a distraction than anything else: Western Alliance is a highly profitable regional banking franchise and should be able to report strong earnings on October 21, 2025.

Estimates imply 21% Y/Y EPS growth in Q3

While investors clearly disliked Wednesday's news about Western Alliance’s exposure to First Brands Group-related receivables, the regional bank is profitable and it should remain so going forward, unless it were to experience a considerable deterioration in asset quality... which would likely only happen in case of a U.S. recession.

The earnings estimate trend so far has been slightly negative... mainly because the regional bank faces prospects for declining net interest income in a falling-rate world. In the last 90 days, analysts have revised their Q3 EPS estimates upwards 0 times which compares to 14 downward revisions. Analysts currently expect $2.07 in adjusted Q3 EPS for Western Alliance which still implies a respectable year-over-year growth rate of ~21%.

WAL’s valuation

Shares of Western Alliance Bancorporation are trading at a premium to book value: currently, investors pay a 33% mark-up to BV, but this does not make the regional bank expensive.

As opposed to the April-June 2023 period, which is when regional banks traded at massive discounts to book value following the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank, most regional banks now trade at premiums to book value.

Western Alliance's shares are still relatively cheap in a historical context: in the last five years, the regional lender achieved, on average, a price-to-book ratio of 1.63X. The industry group average P/B -- which is made up of the price-to-book ratios of U.S. Bancorp (USB), KeyCorp (KEY) and Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB) -- is 1.33X, meaning shares of WAL are currently trading at about the industry group average.

If shares were to revalue to the longer term P/B ratio of 1.63X, shares of Western Alliance could have a fair value in the neighborhood of $101 per-share. This price target could be achieved if the regional lender continues to grow its book value and maintains high bank profitability. My price target, considering a closing price of $82.35 per-share on Wednesday, implies 22% upside revaluation potential.

Risks with WAL

What I consider more of a risk to Western Alliance's financial performance than the leveraged facility provided to Point Bonita Capital is a potential acceleration of the Federal Reserve's rate cut trajectory. The Federal Reserve cut interest rates 25 basis points in September to a new range of 4.00% to 4.25%, which is generally a negative for banks that lend out their money at higher interest rates to borrowers. Further, a deterioration in asset quality remains a long term risk factor for investors, especially if the U.S. economy were to fall into a recession.

Final thoughts

Western Alliance has some exposure to First Brands Group, a bankrupt auto-parts supplier (although no party has quantified this exposure just yet), but it is unlikely that WAL faces a serious financial risk here, in my opinion.

Receivables portfolios tend to be diversified, and Jefferies itself said that it doesn’t see a material risk for investors here. As such, I believe the drop may actually constitute a buying opportunity considering that Western Alliance should be set for a reasonably strong earnings report for the third-quarter.

The bank's consensus EPS estimates indicate 21% year-over-year growth for Q3 and if the regional bank continues to grow its book value, I believe Western Alliance has a solid chance to revalue to my fair value estimate of $101/share.

data.title: The Neglected Asset Class In Fixed Income: Mortgage-Backed Securities
data.description:
The Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index

The primary standard for the fixed income markets is the Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index.

The Aggregate Index, for short, is to the bond market what the S&P 500 is to stocks.

It measures the performance of the broader US investment-grade bond market. The index is comprised of all investment-grade fixed income debt instruments with a remaining maturity of greater than one year. Its component sectors include US Treasuries, Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS) and Corporate Bonds.

When investors think of the bond market, their focal point is frequently US Treasury Securities, as they represent by far the largest segment of Aggregate Index. The benchmark security is the 10-year US Treasury Note.

Treasury Securities comprise 45% of the Aggregate Index, followed by MBS at 24.5%, and Corporate Bonds at 23.9%.

Treasuries get a lot of attention because in addition to their high credit quality and liquidity, there are a lot of them. Treasuries are the fastest growing segment of the bond market.

With the government’s burgeoning fiscal deficits, the US Treasury is constantly issuing new debt so that they can pay the government’s bills. Treasury averages 8 auctions, raising a total of over $50 billion, each week.

Since immediately preceding the Great Financial Crisis (GFC), when our deficit first crossed the $1 trillion threshold, the US Treasury share of the Aggregate Index has steadily climbed from 21% to 45%.

But the bond market is more than just the 10-year Treasury Note.

The Neglected Asset Class: Mortgage-Backed Securities

Mortgage -Backed Securities, on the other hand, have followed the opposite path.

Pre-GFC MBS represented 44% of the Aggregate Index, but now are only 24.5%.

Their share of the market has declined significantly because of the changes to the real estate market post GFC. The GFC was caused, in part, due to the collapse in the real estate market. A large number homeowners defaulted on their mortgages when the value of their home fell below what they owed to the bank.

This wreaked havoc on the Mortgage-Backed Securities market, because these instruments contained the defaulted mortgages. Prices plummeted and liquidity dried up.

The Fed stepped in to deal with the crisis and began purchasing MBS, for the first time ever, through their Quantitative Easing program. As can be seen in orange in the chart below, the Fed went from holding zero MBS in 2007, to being the largest individual owner by the time QE ended in 2022. At that time the Fed’s positions represented 25.3% of the entire MBS market.

MBS issuance slowed dramatically immediately following the GFC, as the real estate market took time to recover.

The following chart shows the monthly net issuance from Ginnie Mae (blue), Fannie Mae (white) and Freddie Mac (orange), the three government agencies that originate MBS. It wasn’t until the pandemic, when the real estate market shifted in the opposite direction, that issuance began rising again.

This was due to the sharp drop in mortgage rates as a result of the Fed’s resumed QE program. The interest rate for a 30-year fixed rate mortgage plummeted to an all-time low 2.65%. This stimulated a wave of refinancing, as homeowners could dramatically reduce their monthly payments on their mortgage. In some cases, they could take out cash from the built -up equity value in their home and still reduce their monthly payment.

In addition, because of the increased work from home practice due to the pandemic, new home sales jumped.

During this period, from 2008-2022, the entire MBS market grew by $3.3 trillion.

The Fed, through their QE programs, purchased $2.7 trillion of MBS. Essentially then, the Fed was the only one interested in the MBS, as they gobbled up 82% of the net new issuance. While the Fed’s market share of MBS was increasing, all other investors in MBS saw their market share decline.

Since the Fed was a program buyer of MBS, and not sensitive to value, they drove spreads to record lows.

The market shifted when the Fed began Quantitative Tightening in early 2022. First, interest rates, including mortgage rates, started rising. Second, not only did the Fed stop buying MBS, but they also started reducing their holdings through QT.

Because the major buyer of MBS was no longer in the market, and rates were rising, spreads began widening again.

Additionally, total MBS outstanding plateaued at the pre-QT level.

This brings us to today.

Relative Value

The widening of spreads has made the MBS market attractive again, on a relative value basis. For the first time since the GFC, MBS are the cheapest they’ve been compared to corporate bonds.

As QT is winding down and the Fed is now in a rate cutting mode, MBS are compelling.

Demand Is Increasing

Demand for bond mutual funds has increased significantly, following the decline in April caused by the Liberation Day tariff scare. This involves the largest mutual funds, including the PIMCO Income Fund, that has received about 1/3 of the new cash investments. They are carrying an overweighted position in MBS.

Mutual Fund exposure to MBS has increased by 43% since QT began to $766 billion. This is their highest level in 12 years.

A surprising source of new demand for MBS has come from the Broker/Dealer community. Their MBS holdings have increased to $627 billion, nearly double their exposure since QT began.

Broker/Dealers are increasing their positions because their trading volume has risen dramatically. Their average daily trading volume of MBS is at the highest level since the record high set during the pandemic. Yet this is occurring with new MBS issuance at 1/3 of what it was then.

Another source of potential demand is from the banking sector. Banks are the largest overall holders of MBS. Their MBS positions have tracked their deposits. While MBS holdings have paused recently, their continued deposit growth, combined with the Fed’s resumed rate cutting may lead to further increases in their MBS positions.

MBS Performance

MBS have been the strongest component of the Aggregate Index this year. The have returned 7.2% YTD, along with Corporate Bonds, compared to the 5.53% return of Treasuries and the 6.39% return of the Aggregate Index YTD.

Additionally, MBS are on track to have their best annual return in 23 years, if the current trend continues through the remainder of the year. The anticipated further Fed rate cuts should help.

Conclusion

Mortgage-Backed Securities have been the neglected asset class in the fixed income market. Since the GFC, which in part was caused by the collapse of the real estate market, MBS have declined from representing 44% of the Aggregate Bond Index to 24.5%.

Treasury supply has increased tremendously with our burgeoning fiscal deficits. MBS issuance, on the other hand, has stalled due to the sharp decline in home sales caused by rising mortgage rates.

As spreads widened when the Fed began tightening, MBS became the cheapest they’ve been, on a relative basis, since the GFC.

This has caused demand to increase from mutual funds and broker/dealers. Additionally, the decline in rates, and growing deposit bases, may cause banks to increase their positions.

The sector has performed well and is on track to have its best year since 2002.

Perhaps Mortgage-Backed Securities will be neglected no more.

data.title: My Dividend Stock Portfolio: New September Dividend Record - 100 Holdings With 13 Buys
data.description:
September was a strong month for U.S. stocks. The S&P 500 rose 3.5%, the Nasdaq jumped 5.6%, and the Dow Jones gained 1.9%, with tech leading the way. The rally was supported by Fed rate cuts, solid economic data, and a strong earnings season.

Big tech companies drove much of the gains. Alphabet (GOOG) climbed after successful AI product launches and favorable antitrust news, while Nvidia (NVDA) continued to dominate the AI hardware market. Meta (META) boosted its advertising and virtual reality platforms through expanded AI integration. These developments helped push the Nasdaq and S&P 500 to new highs and reinforced investor optimism about the technology sector.

Portfolio Changes in September

For the first time in about two years, my net investment activity in September turned negative, at –$1,300, as I decided to partially trim my Broadcom (AVGO) position after an almost surreal run over the past couple of years. I have full conviction in Broadcom but the parabolic rise of AI investments is unlike anything I have ever seen on that magnitude and this clearly can't go on for many more years, at least that is what I think. Needless to say, the stock is still above the level at which I sold, but that’s part of the game. My credo remains: it’s better to lock in some gains now than to risk regretting having waited too long—hindsight is always easy to contemplate.

Beyond investing, September was mostly about focusing on myself. I ran two half marathons and participated in other races, which demanded significant training and recovery. The markets took a clear back seat, and I actually enjoyed the mental break.

The euro continues to rise as the USD weakens, which naturally affects my portfolio and dividend income. I have no clear idea where exchange rates will go in the short, medium, or long term, and frankly there’s little to do except accept the risk if you invest in U.S. stocks as a European. Avoiding USD exposure altogether doesn’t seem realistic, especially given the poor state and prospects of European economies—Germany in particular—thanks to failed policies and misguided ideologies.

With the limited focus, my main picks this month were MidCap Financial Investment Corporation (MFIC), Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL) and Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC).

Altogether, my September purchases added $64 in annual net dividend income—marking the second time this year that I’ve fallen short of my informal goal of boosting monthly income by around $100. I’m aiming to make up for it with a stronger Q4.

I'd like to raise that to $120 or even $150 per month in the coming months, but so far, I've fallen short-partly because yields have compressed a bit, and partly because I've been prioritizing travel over investing lately.

Overall, the average yield on cost of my new investments in September came in at around 8.1%, reinforcing my belief in BDCs as a core part of my portfolio. The markets appear to be pricing in potential dividend cuts for some BDCs as the Fed continues cutting rates, but even if that occurs, it doesn’t shake my long-term conviction. In the short term, we could see further double-digit declines across the BDC sector, and if that happens, I’m happy to step up my purchasing activity. Time will tell whether I’m spot on or simply being overly optimistic.

All these purchases break down as follows:

All net purchases in September can be found below

Dividend Income in September 2025

Dividend income hit a new September record of $1,221 (in EUR the figure is below last year's value demonstrating the enormous and sudden weakening of the dollar) fueled by the growing BDC segment of Ares Capital Corporation, Goldman Sachs BDC, MidCap Financial Investment Corporation and Capital Southwest and the semi-annual dividend from the Commonwealth Bank of Australia (OTCPK:CBAUF). All but one of them are part of my regular investments and will further push quarter-end dividends to new highs moving forward. Outside these four stocks the main dividend growth is coming from organic dividend growth

Looking ahead, I’m confident my March/June/September/December dividend schedule will soon hit $1,300, and I’ve got my sights firmly set on crossing the $1,500 mark within the next two years.

In alignment with my investment strategy, I've incorporated a BDC/Non-BDC breakdown into my dividend income charts. This addition allows for more precise monitoring of this critical development.

Additionally, I have created a new visualization that dissects dividend income into two distinct categories: BDCs and Non-BDCs. These categories in turn are separated by dividend payment frequency into Monthly (blue) and Quarterly (orange) in absolute and relative terms. In Q3 year-to-date, BDCs accounted for 37% of my total dividends. As mentioned earlier, it’s possible that some of these dividends could be cut this year or next, which has me considering whether adjustments to my portfolio allocation are needed. For now, I’m already slowing down purchases to build up more cash, but the question remains whether additional steps should be taken.

This metric is telling and will further guide me as it develops over time.

In September, the dividend income from my top three regular payers reached $460, a solid increase from last year’s average of $390. Barring any dividend cuts, that figure should climb to over $500 by the same time next year, marking another significant milestone.

All this is depicted below:

Here is a chart that shows the development of my net dividend income by month over time between 2015 and 2025. You can see the growth of my dividend income and the average annual dividend for each year:

This chart is my favorite because it illustrates the progression of my dividend income over time and allows me to easily see the average annual dividend for each year.

Next, I have plotted all the individual dividend payments I have received, coloring them by year and arranging the years side by side instead of horizontally as in previous updates. This visualization allows for a more comprehensive analysis of the distribution of my dividend payments over time.

By visualizing the data in this manner, we get a clear picture of how dividend payments are spread out over different years, offering valuable insights into the overall growth of my dividend income over the years.

This visualization is initially quite cluttered, but it contains a wealth of information. It shows every individual dividend payment I have received since I began my investment journey in 2015, represented as a circle that is colored and sized according to its contribution. The view is organized by month and year, allowing for a more comprehensive analysis of the development of my dividend income over time. For each year and month, a white rectangle indicates the average monthly dividend. The area where dividends fall below the average is filled in dark red, while the area above the average is colored dark green.

This type of data visualization allows for a detailed examination of the distribution of my dividend payments over time and enables me to easily identify trends and patterns in my income growth.

Now, zooming in on September, we can immediately see a number of big colorful circles in a sea of black. The bigger the circle and the bigger the distance to the previous circle for the same stock, the bigger is the change in dividend income compared to the prior years.

As mentioned above, BDCs with Ares Capital and MidCap Financial as well as the Commonwealth Bank of Australia are clearly standing out and cementing their dominance.

Apart from that, when it comes to the dividends for 2025, I notice mostly blue circles at the top of the scale, which is exactly what I want to see. These big blue circles represent a rising dividend income, and my goal is to have as many of them as possible at the top of the chart.

Overall, my aim is to observe numerous large orange circles positioned at the highest point on the scale. This signifies a positive trend of increasing dividend income, which is precisely what I'm striving for.

Another way to express the monthly dividend income is in terms of Gifted Working Time (GWT). Assuming an average hourly rate of $37.34 for 2025, my GWT and annual net dividends have been as follows:

  • 2018: 121 hours GWT, $3,000 in annual net dividends
  • 2019: 142 hours GWT, $3,600 in annual net dividends
  • 2020: 152 hours GWT, $3,800 in annual net dividends
  • 2021: 180 hours GWT, $5,050 in annual net dividends
  • 2022: 229 hours GWT, $6,400 in annual net dividends
  • 2023: 272 hours GWT, $8,800 in annual net dividends
  • 2024: 284 hours GWT, $10,200 in annual net dividends
  • 2025: Targeting at least 295 hours GWT, $11,000 in annual net dividends

The overall target for the year with $11,000 in annual net dividends is very conservative, but I expect that I will have to take out some risk from my portfolio as I am eyeing to raise capital for real estate investments if I encounter the right deal and definitely for a lot of traveling. This would represent a growth rate of around 8% which admittedly is a low target but definitely something I am willing to update as the year unfolds. If things go differently, the dream target would be to pass the next milestone of $12,000 which would require around 18% dividend growth. Currently, year-to-date dividend income growth still stands at a surprisingly high 15%, down from the massive 19% recorded at the end of July, as the decline I had expected finally begins to unfold.

That would be a great achievement, but nothing I am banking on to reach this year - it would be the icing on the cake.

Across the years, my YTD Dividend Race has been as follows:

  • 2018: Disappointing
  • 2019: Phenomenal, benefiting from a low baseline in the prior year
  • 2020: Fairly disappointing
  • 2021: Phenomenal
  • 2022: Much better than expected, fueled by the very weak euro
  • 2023: Breathtaking
  • 2024: Simply great

I have updated the YTD Dividend Race chart to show the development of YTD dividend income on a daily basis, allowing us to see that most of my dividend income is generated mid-month and towards the end of the month. The chart also breaks down the development by month for the current year, making it easy to identify significant jumps in income.

Expressed in GWT, it presents itself as follows:

What this shows is as follows:

  1. All time (blue area) - Around 1,720 hours, or 215 days, of active work have been replaced with passive income since the start of my dividend journey. Assuming a five-day workweek, this translates into well more than half a year in total.
  2. Full-year 2025 (green bars) - Around 227 hours, or 28.4 days, of active work have been replaced with passive income in 2025, which is basically equivalent to almost six full working weeks funded with dividends. That is great progress, and I can't wait to add another month to that statistic.
  3. Highlighted in pink is the accumulated total at the end of the current reporting month (September).

This visualization allows us to see the overall impact of my dividend income on the amount of active work I am able to replace with passive income. It also enables us to see the progress made year-to-date, as well as the accumulated total at the end of the current reporting month.

Upcoming October Dividends

As every month, October is packed with dividend payments heavily concentrated around the 15th and at month-end when the dividend checks from Canada's big banks as well as JPMorgan will be arriving over a period of just 3-4 days.

Given the majority of my savings plans feature companies that pay dividends in January such as W. P. Carey, Altria (MO), Cisco Systems (CSCO), JPMorgan, Toronto-Dominion Bank, Bank of Nova Scotia, Stryker (SYK), Medtronic (MDT) as well as ongoing investments into stocks like Rithm Capital (RITM), it surely is going to be a big month that should easily set a new all-time dividend record for the month of October.

The snapshot below is taken from my free-for-all released Dividend Calendar (make sure to follow instructions in the video) and shows my expected dividend payments in October.

Dividend Portfolio

At the end of September, my dividend portfolio is composed as follows:

Company Name Ticker % Market Value Market Value (€)

data.title: Omnicom: Buy While It's Historically Cheap
data.description:
It’s been a while since I last covered Omnicom Group Inc. (NYSE:OMC) back in March, highlighting expected synergies from its pending merger with The Interpublic Group of Companies, Inc. (IPG), along with its solid revenue growth and profitability.

In a market that rewards tech stocks with speculative valuations, OMC continues to trade at a bargain price of $79.76 with a forward P/E of just 9x, which sits far below historical levels, and a 3.6% dividend yield. As shown below, OMC stock has been rangebound in the $70-80 level and currently sits well inside the bottom half of its 52-week price range.

In this article, I revisit OMC including recent business results, and discuss why now is a great time to layer into this quality name while the market is not paying attention, so let’s get started!

Why OMC?

Omnicom is a global advertising and communications leader with a number of iconic agency brands under its umbrella. It’s becoming increasingly data-centric and offers a wide range of services in advertising, precision marketing, retail and digital commerce, branding, and public relations, among others. OMC serves over 5,000 clients in more than 70 countries and generated $15.9 billion in total revenue over the trailing 12 months.

OMC’s pending $13 billion acquisition of IPG has cleared several key hurdles, the most recent of which includes an approval by the UK antitrust regulator. This merger creates not only an advertising behemoth but also gives OMC access to vast amounts of data between the two companies. This enables it to better compete with its peers like WPP plc (WPP) in an increasingly data-centric environment.

Notably, OMC has adapted well to a changing media landscape even on a standalone basis. As shown below, its revenue has solidly grown since hitting a trough during the pandemic in 2021.

OMC demonstrated steady growth during Q2 2025 with organic revenue rising by 3% over the prior-year period. At the same time, adjusted EBITDA rose by 3.7% YoY, resulting in a flat YoY EBITDA margin of 15.3% despite inflationary headwinds. This was driven by cost efficiencies, which led to a respectable 5.1% YoY growth in EPS to $2.05.

Key drivers include the Media & Advertising segment, which posted 8% organic growth due to strong client demand for data-driven media solutions. Additionally, Precision Marketing grew 5%, led by digital and CRM-driven performance. This was offset by weakness in other areas such as Public Relations (-9%) and Healthcare (-5%), which faced softer trends due to client-specific factors and tough comps from last year.

Management is guiding for full-year 3.5% organic revenue growth at the midpoint of range. Looking ahead, the integration of IPG is expected to unlock new opportunities for cross-selling, data monetization, and global efficiency gains. This includes an expected $750 million in annual run-rate cost synergies. OMC is also quickly adopting an AI agentic framework, which now orchestrates multiple AI agents across campaign workflows. This drives efficiency and unlocks opportunities, as noted during the last earnings call:

Over the last year, we have been aggressively and systematically rolling out AI agents throughout our workflows, where we can deploy multiple AI agents that collaborate seamlessly to deliver comprehensive solutions rather than isolated AI tools addressing individual tasks, we can now orchestrate intelligent agents across campaign life cycles, simultaneously analyzing data, optimizing strategies and refining creative elements.

This capability is powered by a proprietary data asset and institutional knowledge, democratizing access to our industry-leading consumer intelligence, encompassing behaviors, demographics, cultural insights and transactions.

OMC’s investments in AI could help it gain a competitive edge and capture market share. This is further supported by a growing TAM (total addressable market). As shown below, global digital ad spend is expected to grow at a 10.2% CAGR between now and 2033 to $1.55 billion, driven by social media, video, and search engine spend.

Importantly, OMC is supported by a strong balance sheet with a BBB+ credit rating from S&P. It has $3.3 billion in cash on hand and has a safe net debt-to-TTM EBITDA ratio of 1.5x. This supports the 3.6% dividend yield, which is well covered by a 34% payout ratio and comes with a 5-year CAGR of 6.1%.

The balance sheet also provides a solid foundation for robust capital returns, as reflected by $223 million of stock buybacks in Q2 and an expected $600 million in total buybacks for the full year. OMC has reduced its share count by 10% over the past 5 years and 19% over the past 10, as shown below.

Lastly, I see value in OMC at the current price of $79.76 with a forward P/E ratio of 9.3, which equates to a robust 10.7% earnings yield on every dollar spent on share buybacks. As shown below, OMC currently trades at a significant discount to its historical P/E of 15.1.

At the current valuation, the market appears to be overly rotated on merger execution jitters and potential disruptive effects of AI on the industry. However, chances are that OMC will emerge as a stronger company post-merger, and its investments in AI could drive meaningful top and bottom-line growth for the company.

Sell-side analysts who follow the company estimate 9-10% EPS growth annually over the next 2 years, which I believe is reasonable for the aforementioned reasons. This, combined with the dividend yield and potential for a reversion to the mean valuation could result in total returns in the mid to high teens.

Risks to OMC include the potential for a macroeconomic downturn due to tariffs or other factors. As such, clients could slash marketing budgets and that would negatively affect OMC. Moreover, competitors, big and small, could also leverage AI to woo clients away from OMC by providing a better cost proposition. As such, OMC would need to prove the value of its agentic AI to clients in order to build or maintain market share.

Investor Takeaway

Omnicom presents a compelling opportunity for value investors, with it trading at just 9.3x forward earnings and offering a well-covered 3.6% dividend yield. Despite merger-related noise and AI disruption fears, OMC continues to execute well. This is reflected by steady organic growth, expansion of data-driven capabilities, and the rolling out of a sophisticated AI agentic framework.

OMC’s pending IPG merger could be transformative. This could stem from unlocking significant synergies, expanding data assets, and boosting competitive positioning in a growing digital ad market. Lastly, it has strong fundamentals, disciplined capital returns, and a discounted valuation well below historical norms. As such, OMC looks positioned to deliver potentially attractive total returns in the coming years.

data.title: ADX: A Solid Growth Income Fund
data.description:
Investors may have some confusion about the emerging option income ETFs. These funds typically sell covered calls to generate income based on an underlying portfolio that is heavily growth-exposed to the Magnificent 7 companies. The key concern for the income investors is about the capping effect of these funds, which may not provide the capital growth as expected. Adams Diversified Equity Fund (NYSE:ADX) is a good case study for an alternative approach to acquiring growth income. The CEF fund offers a dividend of over 8%, a reasonable expense ratio, and possesses some key features for a growth portfolio. I have a buy rating on ADX and recommend the fund to long-term investors looking for market-beating total returns.

ADX Overview

ADX is a CEF from Adams Funds, focusing on the total return and also a high income yield at a minimum of 8% of its average NAV, with about 2% paid quarterly. The fund started very early in 1929 and has developed an AUM of about $2.8B right now.

Although the fund claims that it is investing in "a broadly diversified equity portfolio," The portfolio looks more like a trimmed version of the S&P 500 index. The following is a comparison of the top holdings of ADX and SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY).

One can see that 7 of the top 10 holdings are the same names, with similar weights. The different holdings in the top spots are minor and account for less than 6% altogether. Notice the total concentration is at a similar level, e.g., around 40% even though the total number of holdings is only 96 for ADX.

The sector breakdown shown below is remarkably close. One may not even realize the difference by just glancing through it. The top sectors include technology (about 35%), Financials (13%), Consumer Cyclical, and Communication, which are in the 9-10% range. I believe the settings like this become a de facto standard for the US equity market.

On the other hand, the key factor of 96 total holdings, which is about 1/5 of 5&P 500 holdings, seems to be the alpha edge and has helped ADX to beat the SPY handily in total returns over time. As shown below, in the past 10 years, ADX had a total return of 355% while SPY's total return was only 233% during the same time span.

The fund is relatively cheap for a CEF, charging a 0.59% expense ratio, which is pretty low in the CEF space.

The dividend is over 8% with a dividend growth rate averaging 18.73%for the last 5 years. This is perhaps the best news for the income investors who are seeking a growth portfolio like the S&P 500 for a strong total return in the long term.

Acquire Income from Investment

Growth aside, the key question for the income-focused investment is how to get the money out of the portfolio. To that end, the option income ETFs have become popular among investors because the option premiums from the covered calls are used as the income stream for these funds. I have a few articles covering some ETFs, including this one with an S&P 500 underlying.

However, investors seem to have concerns and confusion about how the option strategy of writing calls is interpreted in terms of investment returns. Some analysis articles in SA even suggest that the substantial "NAV Erosion" could be involved. The key confusion here is that the NAV decrease of the fund does not equal NAV erosion. The distribution itself is not really a NAV erosion. But it is worth noting that it is not easy to manage the call options due to the time-sensitive decay effects. For option-savvy investors, the DIY option is a more economical way to derive the income.

Perhaps a much harder issue for investors is how to get the position back once called away during a bull market run. This is where the ADX fund offers a nice and simple solution. The fund will secure some profit from the capital gains on the portfolio and pass the profit to the shareholders. So the capped-gain issue due to the called-away position from the option income ETF is avoided

Strike the right balance for long-term investing

It may appear to be a simple decision to sell equity using simple math based on how much you can sell to get income. However, selling is known to be a difficult investment decision because my own experience in the past has shown that the selling activities have often been more likely driven by emotions like fear or regret, which could lead to poor, impulsive decisions. In essence, it is a market-timing decision, which is particularly true for the growth names. Selling too early during a bull run may mean missing the upside potential. This would look just like a different version of the gapping effect from the covered call.

The managed distribution from ADX and other similar funds could be a simple solution for individual investors. It largely comes down to the management process deployed by the fund. Investors can leave the hard and emotional trading decisions to the professional fund management. Typically, they can do a better job at selling and deciding how much to take in gain and pass the income to the shareholders as the distribution. The investors, on the other hand, will be able to spend more time and devote their effort to deciding which funds to invest in based on their income needs and long-term investment objectives.

Notice that ADX CEF has paid the dividend as a long-term capital gain (LCG in short) in its recent records. The investors will not need to worry about the holding time to satisfy the LCG requirement. This also makes it easier to change the fund or swap it with a better fund, etc.

As mentioned earlier, I have also been constructing my growth income portfolio using option income ETFs. The combination of both types of income funds could help increase income stability and diversify the overall portfolio.

The Income Fund is used as a wise hedge.

After April's V-shaped recovery of the US equity market, the growth names, such as the Magnificent 7 growth companies, have propelled the market to many new all-time highs in 2025. As a result, the S&P 500 is well in the double-digit gain year-to-date, already exceeding its long-term average growth rate of around 10%.

I believe it is time to expect some form of slowdown (correction) or perhaps sideways market moves for the rest of 2025. Either way, it makes sense to consider hedging against such market headwinds. Switching to the growth income fund could be a smart approach. On one hand, we can take profit off the table by collecting the high distributions and holding off on the reinvesting/DRIP option. On the other hand, some distributions can be used as the capital for opening hedge positions if one expects a big correction that could come on the way. Furthermore, the income accumulated may allow investors to do sector rotation or buy-in dip and take new positions on the more attractive names identified.

Risk Analysis

The ADX portfolio illustrates a typical growth exposure pattern with the top-tier US stocks. The common risks for growth names should apply, including the high concentration (40% for the top 10 holdings). Due to the smaller size, the fund would expect bigger corrections or drawdowns from the market headwinds, such as the growth scare and the slowing US economy.

Investors should also be fully aware of the other risks associated with the risky growth assets, such as high volatility, etc. The hedging and risk protection measures are highly recommended during the bear market and other market volatile times.

Closing Thoughts

How to collect income for the growth investment may not be an easy decision. ADX CEF offers a good alternative solution. Investors can get 8% yield and expect a growing portfolio beating the S&P 500 in total returns for the long term. Such an outcome may be superior to many of the option income funds, which could charge a higher expense ratio and experience issues like NAV erosion and gain limitation. ADX is a solid long-term buy for income investors who want to build a different growth income tool to broaden their income sources.

data.title: The Opportunity I Waited For: Ferrari Crashes 16% And I Am Buying (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Ferrari held its Capital Markets Day earlier today. While I am writing this article, I am seeing the stock plummet. It was down 7% when I first started reading the two press releases, and now the stock has been suspended from trading because it was down 16%. Big spoiler: I am ready to buy as soon as the trading activity resumes. After all, we rarely see such an opportunity for such a stock, and I would like to walk you through the reasons I see to say this.

A guy I met a few days ago told me, "Oh, I know you, you are the Ferrari guy on Seeking Alpha." Well, while I won't claim what is not mine, I admit that I have covered the stock extensively since it was on Ferrari that I published my first article here (we are up 130% since then, vs. the market's 68%). Now, since I don't want to waste your time, you can easily access the 20 articles I have written on the stock. Let me summarize really quickly the main ideas that support my investment, just to give you a framework of how my thought process works on this stock.

In short, the main idea is that Ferrari is a highly predictable company. And I think that the stock is moving down today because investors are not correctly assessing this aspect. There are a few reasons that support Ferrari's predictability. First of all, the company doesn't conceive of itself as a carmaker but as one of the leading luxury brands in the world. Luxury would make you think of fashion and volatility. But Ferrari targets ultra-high-net-worth individuals, who usually keep growing and never have real financial problems. But the way Ferrari appeals to this extremely wealthy niche of customers is by making their mouths water, so to speak, until they finally own one. In fact, around 75% of its sales go to existing customers, which means that people who want to buy a brand new Ferrari have to wait a long time. Secondly, Ferrari keeps its volumes constrained intentionally (right now, less than 14,000 cars per year) so that its exclusivity is strengthened. This leads to an incredibly dynamic second-hand Ferrari market, which Ferrari is able to control both through its dealers and through its services. Third, Ferrari has just informed us that it has achieved a 100% personalization rate on its sales, meaning that each of its cars is somewhat unique. Personalization is the new high-margin revenue stream that Ferrari has explored in the past few years, delivering a profit mastery lesson that I have often highlighted. Finally, thanks to its two-year order book visibility, Ferrari rarely misses earnings estimates and is actually able to gradually revise upward its guidance and beat it.

Ferrari Capital Markets Day's Update

Why is the stock falling so awfully today? Part of this move could be a typical "buy the rumor, sell the news." There could also be some profit-taking since the stock has done so well in the past few years. But let's see the possible negative news.

The first one is here. In its strategic plan press release, Ferrari unveiled its sales mix by engine: in 2030, it will have a lineup of 40% ICE, 40% hybrid, and 20% electric. This means the EV target has been revised down from the 40% goal that was announced a few years ago. As Barron's commented, Ferrari is "the latest high-end automaker to water down its previous EV target." Is this a real problem? Not really. In fact, it actually shows that Ferrari's prudent strategy has allowed it not to waste money on massive EV projects, as many legacy automakers have. In the meantime, it has been able to develop convincing hybrid models. Moreover, Ferrari admitted that the current battery technology available doesn't allow it to build a supercar. This means that we will see an electric Ferrari as part of the main model portfolio.

Secondly, in the same press release, we learned something that I had long been waiting for regarding personalization:

100% of our clients’ cars are uniquely personalized, reflecting a tiered and bespoke approach that ensures every example is one of a kind. This aspect will be further enhanced by the opening of new Tailor Made centers in Tokyo and Los Angeles in 2027, as well as by our new paint shop due to open in 2027.

This doesn't mean that personalization has been saturated. It actually means that Ferrari has learned how to make each car unique to the point that this justifies the opening of two new centers in key markets such as Japan and the U.S. To me, this sounds like more profits coming in.

Third, Ferrari disclosed some key numbers of its ecosystem that were not previously available. We now know that there are around 330k Ferraris that have been manufactured and that around 300k of them are still in existence. What does this imply? Services, maintenance, and extended warranty are among the first things that come to mind:

Since the Company’s founding, Ferrari has produced approximately 330,000 vehicles, over 90% of which are still in existence today and require our constant care. Indeed, customer service is an equally important pillar, delivered through a global network of 180 authorized dealers who undergo regular training and evaluation, and who will soon adopt a refreshed corporate identity. Among the services offered, maintenance and warranty are extended and standardized across all powertrains, covering the car’s entire lifespan. This commercial strategy has been rewarded with increasing client loyalty, with now 90,000 active clients and approximately 32,300 new clients acquired for our brand since 2022. In addition, 45% of our collectors are new since 2022, while the number of Ferrari sports cars owned by collectors has grown by 20%.

Ferrari has increased the number of its active unique clients by 20% in just four years. How has it been able to do so if it keeps being so exclusive? Through its used vehicle network. This has been leveraged as the way to enter into the ecosystem and be part of it. Collectors are also growing their desire to enrich their garages thanks to the strategy of four new models released per year.

Commercially, I see a brand that is growing stronger.

Let's look at the financials, then. Perhaps we will see something that doesn't work well.

In the financial targets press release, we first find further evidence of what I believe is a key cornerstone of how we should model Ferrari's earnings. In short, Ferrari is usually able to deliver well and beat estimates. In this case, we learned that:

Ferrari exceeded its 2026 profitability targets one year in advance, revising upward its 2025 guidance to net revenues above Euro 7.1B, adj. EBITDA above Euro 2.72B (margin above 38.3%), adj. operating profit of at least Euro 2.06B (margin of 29%), and adj. EPS above Euro 8.80. Industrial FCF should be above Euro 1.3B (1B at the end of last year).

This means that when we now look at the 2026-2030 plan, we should know that the likelihood that Ferrari repeats itself and achieves its targets one year ahead of the plan is there.

So, what has Ferrari announced as its 2030 targets?

The goal is to reach total revenues of Euro 9.0 billion (2025-2030 CAGR 5%), driven by the new product mix and personalizations. The 2030 EBITDA should be at least Euro 3.6 billion (margin above 40%). Ferrari should also reach EBIT of Euro 2.75 in 2030, with a margin above 30%. The main driver this time won't be volume, but mix and price. This ensures that Ferrari's next few years will not dilute the company's exclusivity.

Industrial FCF generation will grow, and Ferrari aims to accumulate around Euro 8B in the next five years. In the meantime, it should spend Euro 4.7B in capex, leaving over Euro 3.3B as true FCF.

Below, you can see the new 2030 guidance.

We also learned that Ferrari's shareholder returns will be increased. The dividend payout ratio moves from 35% to 40% of adj. net profit, with 2025 as the base floor. From 2027 to 2031, Ferrari should distribute approximately Euro 3.5B in dividends while starting a new share repurchase program worth Euro 3.5B.

Now that we know the numbers, we can make some back-of-the-envelope calculations.

Valuation

First of all, I will model my personal forecast of Ferrari's main financials through 2030. As I have said, I strongly believe Ferrari has underpromised to then overdeliver. My 2025 revenue estimate is Euro 7.15B and I expect Ferrari's sales to grow at 6.5% annually. As you will see, Ferrari will hit its target in 2029 and will come close to 10B in 2030 sales. I think this is the real goal: double-digit revenue by 2030. In the EPS calculation, I start from 178M shares outstanding, and consider the buyback program worth 5.6% of the current market cap after the stock plummeted. I consider that Ferrari will reduce its shares outstanding by around 1.2M units per year. It means the buyback will add around 1% per year of EPS growth without considering revenue growth or margin expansion. I assume a stable net income margin of 23% through the next five years.

In Euro B, except for EPS 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030
sales 7.61 8.11 8.64 9.2B 9.8B
Ebitda (%) 2.72B (38.5%) 3.16 (39%) 3.41 (39.5% 3.68 (40%) 4.00 (40.5%)
Ebit 2.05 (29%) 2.37 (29.25%) 2.34 (29.5%) 2.76 (30%) 3.00 (30.25%)
Ind. FCF 1.5 1.85 2.15 2.5 2.9
EPS 9.90 10.62 11.40 12.23 13.01

This means that we have an implied 2030 PE of 27. For next year, the implied PE is 35; it is 33 for 2027, it stays just above 30 when we consider the 2028 estimate, and it moves to 28.7 for the year 2029. Since I consider Ferrari a long-term holding, I see that it rarely sells this cheap. Ferrari will often trade with a forward earnings multiple above 40, if not even 50, which reflects the confidence investors have in its prospects.

Right now, Ferrari is a clear opportunity, and I am increasing my stake.

data.title: Dell's AI Payday Just Got Way Bigger - By 2x
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The market for AI servers is one of the toughest areas to compete in within the AI infrastructure ecosystem.

Competition is fierce, inventory management is a nightmare, and margins are extremely tight, leaving very little room for error. In my opinion, these kinds of setups don't always tend to offer investors alpha-generating opportunities.

However, Dell Technologies Inc. (NYSE:DELL) is one of those companies that stands out for lots of reasons. The Texas-based global technology giant has forged deep partnerships on both ends of the spectrum, with vendors like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and hypergrowth customers like CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV), raising its AI prospects. Its PC and storage business is set for inflection with those end markets on the mend. And the company is seeing such robust growth in its AI server business that it 2x-ed its long-term growth forecasts at the recently held 2025 Analyst Day.

The significant increase in Dell’s forward-looking long-term estimates denotes the strong AI momentum the company is expecting to benefit from as demand for its AI servers keeps surging.

I am reiterating my Buy rating and raising my PT on Dell.

AI Is Fast-Forwarding Dell’s Growth Prospects

I have written about Dell a few times already, and I advised investors to ignore the extreme focus that markets have on the company’s margin profile. Like I mentioned earlier, margin expansion is extremely important for the markets when it comes to expectations from Dell and its peers in the AI server market. This is because most of the players in this space, including Dell, tend to operate their business on mid-single operating margins, which are razor-thin.

Given the vast opportunities that demand for AI infrastructure provides for raising the operating leverage, generally speaking,

Markets tend to think that the elevated demand for AI infrastructure buildouts should lead to stronger operating leverage, which generally is the case, except for the AI server end market.

General expectations in the market were for Dell’s ISG segment (Infrastructure Solutions Group) to guide/report full-year FY26 operating margins of ~11.5%. The deceleration in Dell ISG’s operating margins to 8.8% in Q2 FY26 complicated that path to ~11.5% operating margins, and markets seemed not to like that one bit, with Dell’s shares selling off after the Q2 report.

The gross margin deceleration to 18% from the usual ~22% quarterly gross margin levels rubbed more salt into the wounds of Dell’s shares. Markets ignored the accelerating 19.6% y/y growth in Q2 consolidated revenues and the 44% jump in Q2 ISG revenues. (That’s how focused markets are on Dell and other server players' margin expansion).

However, Dell surprised investors with its 2025 Analyst Day, from Tuesday this week, where management made some strong projections about the growth runways that lie ahead for the company, especially for its AI servers business and the possible margin accretion that investors can expect ahead.

Let’s start with Dell’s ISG business outlook and navigate to their consolidated outlook that will eventually move the needle on its valuation.

Management believes Dell ISG’s revenues will swell to $55.3B in FY26 this year, a 27% jump from the previous year. Investors can already see in the chart below how revenues generated from shipping AI servers, like its PowerEdge AI servers, have propelled Dell’s ISG revenue growth trajectory beyond the 8% CAGR.

The boost that Dell got from selling AI servers is significant. Management revealed they expect to generate $20B from selling their PowerEdge AI servers this year, implying a staggering 253% CAGR in the last two years. Dell is outselling the market for AI servers, which is expected to grow 45% this year, according to IDC’s latest figures.

Dell also indicated, in the 2025 Analyst Day meeting, that margins for its ISG group will land between 10-14%, higher than Wall Street’s expectations of 11.5%. I noted previously. There are two key growth levers Dell is leaning on to raise the operating leverage.

First is Dell IP—the company’s homegrown solutions for storage & proprietary server designs that can help Dell capture more value and avoid reliance on third-party vendors. This will be a key differentiator for Dell, allowing it to work more closely with its deep-pocketed customers and helping it compete with fast-growing Asian server makers like Foxconn, Wistron, etc.

Second is that they see growth in the enterprise segment of their customer base. At the moment, their AI server sales are heavily skewed towards hyperscalers and frontier LLM makers like OpenAI and xAI. But doing business with such an innovative roster of clients is raising the credibility profile of Dell among the enterprise market. Dell revealed that it sold AI servers to more than 3000 unique customers last year and has a line of sight to sell to 6700 enterprise customers.

What Dell’s AI business has done for the company is that it has doubled the expectations that management now has, from growing towards a long-term target range of 3-4% to now growing towards a 7-9% long-term target range. Also, as seen below, even management’s EPS long-term growth expectations have doubled to 15%.

Investors can also go to Seeking Alpha’s Earnings Revisions tool and see how markets had to meaningfully amp up their expectations on Dell because they were behind (or pessimistic) in their outlook.

Markets were expecting Dell to grow consolidated FY27 revenues by ~7% to $115.2B, but the new long-term expectations Dell set pushed markets to rethink their own long-term expectations. Consensus estimates for FY27 revenue expectations have now been raised to $117.2B, up ~9% y/y, while the market’s FY26 revenue expectations have now settled at $107.7B, up 12.7% y/y.

My PT for Dell Indicates Upside Next Year

In my strong opinion, the case for being bullish on Dell should not be ignored.

Keeping management’s new outlook in mind, I concur with the market's current expectations of Dell growing its revenues 12.7% in FY26 and 9% in FY27. Dell is also expected to grow its EPS by ~17.2% per year in FY26 and FY27. For such growth rates, I believe a forward PE of 18-19x is warranted because that is what markets have typically valued the company at.

Currently, Dell is trading at 17.2x FY26 EPS, and it could be reaching the upper end of its fair value range for now. Therefore, comparing 17.2 vs. 18-19x implies about 4.7-9% further upside in Dell’s shares.

However, if investors are considering the next year, which is what markets are doing as we enter the final quarter of 2025, Dell looks attractive. As seen above, Dell’s shares trade at 14.7x FY27 EPS, which is also expected to grow ~17.2%, mirroring FY26’s EPS growth expectations. This implies a ~22-25% upside in Dell’s shares over the course of FY27 or CY26 or a PT of ~$197 per share.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

My bullish thesis on Dell is based on my analysis of Dell’s forward-looking long-term projections. This is also dependent on Dell actually hitting their margin projections of 10-14% as stated. However, the market can get tougher, as I noted at the start of this post, which could impede Dell from raising its margin profile, especially in the ISG business that accounts for AI server shipments.

Additionally, Dell’s PC business has finally started reporting some growth after years of sales contractions. If PC sales reverse to the downside, expect pressure on the overall sales and margins.

Takeaway

Dell’s efforts in capturing demand in the AI server market have elevated the growth outlook for the company and pushed it out of the meager low single-digit growth rates investors were used to seeing pre-ChatGPT to high single-digit growth rates investors can now expect.

Versus management’s newly minted growth outlook, Dell’s shares look very attractive. I have raised my PT on Dell, and I reiterate my Buy rating.

data.title: Joby Aviation: Shares Fall After Capital Raise, A Necessary Step Toward eVTOL Commercialization
data.description:
Joby Aviation, Inc. (NYSE:JOBY) stock tumbled 9% following a $514 million capital raise. The stock is now down 5.5% since my last report in which I marked Joby Aviation as a Buy. In my view, capital raises such as the one announced are part of the game for developers of eVTOL in an effort to transform urban mobility. In this report, I discuss the capital raise and attach a price target to Joby Aviation stock.

Capital Raise In Support Of eVTOL Certification And Manufacturing

While share prices have fallen since the announcement of the capital raise, I believe this is the ordinary course of business for eVTOL developers. Opportunities for urban air mobility are huge, but the reality is that developing an eVTOL is capital-intensive. For many companies that aim to capitalize on a transformative technology or development, it currently seems that the sky is the limit. That, however, undermines the reality that for many companies there is no revenue, let alone cash flow, to fund the development, and accessing the debt markets to raise funds is often also not an option. That leaves equity raises as the only realistic path to fund development. It is also not the case that with having an eVTOL built or developed that the capitalization runway starts. Before that, the certification path is part of the development and the initial production of the early models is likely going to be loss-making. So, capital raises right now are the only solution and that means as a shareholder you have to accept that risk or time your position well to minimize dilution risk.

Joby Aviation will be offering 30.5 million shares priced at $16.85 with gross proceeds of $513.9 million. On top of that there are options to raise an additional $77.1 million by offering an additional 4.575 million shares. The offering happened at an 11% discount, which I believe is actually not a bad discount at all. Proceeds will be used to fund certification and manufacturing. So, it is really the playbook capital raise I would say.

The Impact On Joby Aviation Stock Price Targets

Modeling the dilution to the share price is actually not very straightforward. Ideally, I use an EV/EBITDA valuation method. That is already where we run into some issues as the company is expected to produce EBITDA losses of around $500 million annually through 2027. So, better-suited methods to model everything are through EV/Sales. However, that also comes with a bit of a drawback as the company is not expected to ramp up sales until 2027. Therefore, whatever sales projection we end up with for 2027, it is just the start of the revenue ramp.

If we apply a price-to-book valuation at 16x P/B and assume that the optional 4.575 million shares will also be issued, we end up with around 12% upside taking the lowest upside figure between 2025 and 2027. Absent revenues and EBITDA, the P/B also appears inflated. We note that Joby Aviation traded around 5-6 times P/B, which is now substantially higher. So, the price-to-book is not an extremely good indicator either. In fact, if we put the price-to-book at two times the median for Joby, we get to 16-20 percent downside. Perhaps that downside provides us with a bit of an indication of when Joby Aviation stock becomes a strong buy.

At 6x EV/book value, which is roughly where the company used to trade, there is 45-50 percent downside, and at 12x EV/book value, we note that Joby Aviation is more or less fairly valued. So, the capital raise has not opened a major opportunity from a value perspective. To me, it is an ordinary capital raise that fits in the company’s commercialization path and I do expect that capital raises will be needed for the next few years.

Conclusion: Capital Raise Was Nothing Exceptional For Joby Aviation

The stock price did tank on the news of the capital raise, but I consider it to be the most logical path at this point to raise funds as eVTOL developers prepare their businesses for certification of their products and production ramp-up. With that ramp-up in mind and the associated capital requirements, I do believe additional capital raises will be required and at 12x P/B, the stock is more or less fairly valued for this year and I keep my speculative buy rating. The impact of future capital raises does depend on the prevailing market prices for the common stock and the discount offered on additional offerings, but to me, the $514 million capital raise was one that followed the trajectory of the company.

data.title: Arthur J. Gallagher & Co.: Fairly Valued Global Insurance Broker
data.description:
Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. (NYSE:AJG) is a global specialty insurance broker. With uncertainty about tariffs, interest rates, inflation, and many other macroeconomic stressors, many investors look to insurance, and brokers provide the profits without the underwriting risks.

While AJG has been an all-weather grower and has more growth ahead, it's possible to overpay for this, and so it's not quite a Buy today.

Business Model

AJG does not purely engage in brokerage. The two revenue segments consist of:

  • Brokerage: 86% of 2024 Revenue
  • Risk Management: 14% of 2024 Revenue

As the table above from the latest Form 10K shows, brokerage revenues are not necessarily all commissions from the policies they directly sell. Almost a third of revenue is fees they garner not just from their risk management services (where they provide data and analytics to underwriters) but from fees on customer service for underwriters, as well as a retail platform used by other brokers (giving AJG wholesale revenue).

The table above illustrates only about half of brokerage revenues comes from their own direct commissions. It also shows that about 38% of this revenue is from international markets, which are primarily the UK, Australia, Canada, and New Zealand.

Across these geographies, they sell dozens of lines of P&C insurance.

The third segment, which doesn't produce revenue but is an important part of their model, is corporate. I wouldn't normally stress such a segment, but AJG regularly engages in M&A, and this is a major focus of corporate on a regular basis. CEO J. Patrick Gallagher explained in the Q2 earnings call:

Our machine, and we're in just a great position is driven by literally hundreds of people in the field around the world, talking to folks that they admire and working with people who have been hired by folks to sell their business. And we're on that short list of virtually anybody that wants to take a look at possibly selling their enterprise, large or small. Our tuck-in acquisition business purchases are not all $50 million, $100 million. There's lots of 2s, 5s, 10s. These are family businesses, and they're not PE roll-ups and they're looking for a home for their people.

As a brokerage firm is more effective at scale, M&A is an important way to win and keep business, while increasing margins over time.

Financial History

How has such a model treated the company over time? Well, as one might expect of M&A, it's contributed steady growth.

Between 2015 and 2024, annual revenue more than doubled, from $5.3B to $10.9B. This is great for any business, but much how much money finds its way to shareholders?

Above, I've displayed the impact to free cash flow, as well as adjusted FCF that factors in M&A activity, which is visible in every year of the past decade with a lower AFCF figure. Some years even show a net outflow.

Some of this gap has been plugged by issuance of debt. Q2 results showed a long-term debt balance of $12B. While this seems covered by $14.3B in cash, this is not quite the whole case. As they disclosed in their annual and quarterly reports, $8.5B in cash was raised in December 2024 and another $1.28B in January were raised for the acquisition of AssuredPartners, which occurred in August.

As far as what actually gets back to shareholders, most of this is done through dividends, with very limited buybacks in their history. They have steadily increased cash dividends each year, even in years with outflows.

The year 2025 has shown continued growth. Revenue for Q2 was up 16% YoY, across the entire business.

On the cash flow side, half-year results reflect how timing of M&A can impact things. Full-year results are more indicative of AJG's ability to generate cash.

Overall, it's a steady business, one with consistent growth over many years. It's not terribly risky, but one has to question how much growth, organic and through M&A, is still on the table.

Outlook and Valuation

What makes AJG tempting is that it is a solid business, but with a $79B market cap, it is quite large for an insurance broker, and I think we must temper our assumptions about growth. First, let's look at the business metrics with AssuredPartners acquired, per their company update.

The acquisition adds $3B of TTM revenue to AJG's own $12B of TTM revenue. FCF is recently about 20% of total revenue, suggesting that annual FCF going forward should be about $3B. From there it's a matter of how much we want to anticipate more outflows for M&A.

In the post-acquisition conference call, Gallagher noted the following about the pipeline:

Our global pipeline of opportunities with term sheets signed delivered or being prepared includes nearly 40 potential mergers with about $500 million of annualized revenue. So the pipeline remains strong and full of tuck-in merger and acquisition opportunities around the globe that can contribute to our long-term strategic growth.

He went on to add this market data:

The Swiss Re Institute estimates there is more than $7 trillion of annual insurance premiums globally, $4 trillion alone in non-life premium, and global non-life premiums are growing annually due to economic expansion, emerging risks and exposures, increasing insurance demand and new coverages purchased by those that already buy insurance.

And added:

It's estimated there are tens of thousands of agencies and brokerage firms across our 5 major geographies, including 30,000 in the U.S. alone according to a leading consulting firm. Most of these firms have less than $25 million in annual revenue. Our family owned and have salespeople and colleagues with strong roots in their local communities.

For this reason, I think it's likely that growth will continue, organically and inorganically, as it has the past decade. During Q2 and the update calls, management guided for high, single-digit premium increases. With the global growth trends in specialty insurance that they noted, as well as improving margins as they scale, I think double-digit FCF growth is possible, particularly as they integrate AssuredPartners.

I'll value AJG with a Discounted Cash Flow calculation under the following assumptions then:

  • $2B as baseline FCF (trimming from $3B to factor some regular M&A)
  • 15% CAGR first 5 years
  • 10% CAGR the next 5 (less cost savings after Assured is integrated)
  • Terminal multiple of 15 (pricing for moderate growth in distant future)
  • Discount rate of 10% (typical return of a market index)

These are very healthy growth assumptions for a business as large as theirs, and I think the recent price near $310 at the $79B market cap can offer positive returns over time but perhaps not favorably in comparison to an index.

Their own data show how premium growth (which increases their commissions) isn't always positive. In periods of economic distress, it's negative. While they have avoided the deepest lows of the average, they face some cyclicality that can slow growth. As these acquisitions are normally financed with debt, I think it's fair to avoid being over-eager on a stock like this.

AJG isn't the only insurance broker growing through M&A, and they will face competition as other players consolidate. I think investors would get an entry price that braces for some of these headwinds. While they don't bear underwriting risk, paying too much create just as much risk to long-term returns.

That's I'm considering it fairly valued for now and waiting for a better discount.

Conclusion

AJG executes its M&A strategy well. It's easy to love insurance, especially the distributor side, but the trick is not to overpay for the growth ahead. While brokers are capital-light compared to insurance, that's not completely true if they're doing leveraged buyouts. Because I want more of a discount to ensure the returns are good and risk is smaller, I rate AJG a Hold for now.

data.title: Why Energy Prices Are Finally Set To Fall Next Year
data.description:
By Warren Patterson

Further OPEC+ supply sees oil surplus grow

Oil prices have been choppy over the past month, with Brent trading briefly above US$70/bbl on the back of geopolitical and sanction risks building in the market. But the move was short-lived, and the market came under renewed pressure following growing noise that OPEC+ will bring further supply onto the market more aggressively than anticipated.

However, with the market already set to move into a large surplus from the fourth quarter of this year and remain there throughout 2026, we do not believe that additional supply from OPEC+ is needed. This gives us growing confidence in our view that oil prices will move lower next year, and we're maintaining our forecast of Brent averaging US$57/bbl.

OPEC+’s recent actions indicate the group remains focused on reclaiming market share. With fiscal breakeven oil prices significantly above current levels, the sustainability of this strategy is likely to come under greater scrutiny in 2026.

There are clear upside risks to the oil market, with the most prominent being the ongoing threat of sanctions and secondary tariffs targeting Russia. Currently, only India faces secondary tariffs for its purchases of Russian oil. However, a more aggressive stance from President Trump could place broader Russian supply at risk. Another key upside risk also stems from Russia on the supply side, specifically the potential for disruptions caused by Ukrainian drone attacks on Russian energy infrastructure. While most of these attacks have focused on refineries, there have been instances targeting port infrastructure, which could directly impact crude oil exports.

Weak Asian LNG demand takes pressure off European gas prices

European gas prices have remained largely under pressure. TTF continues to trade in the low EUR 30s despite Europe moving closer towards the 25/26 heating season. A recovery in Norwegian gas flows following the end of scheduled maintenance would have helped ease any supply concerns. In addition, Asian LNG demand, particularly from China, remains weak, which has helped ensure adequate supply for Europe as we approach the heating season.

EU gas storage ended September at more than 83% full, and with storage targets for the EU now more flexible, we should not expect upward pressure from buying to ensure the 90% storage target is met by 1 November. Ultimately, the outlook for natural gas prices through the winter is going to largely depend, of course, on the weather.

We expect European natural gas prices to trend lower through 2026 and particularly through 2027. The start-up of significant LNG export capacity should ensure that the market becomes increasingly well supplied.

The ramp-up of new additional LNG export capacity should also make it possible for the EU to go ahead with its plan to phase out Russian gas, without putting significant upward pressure on prices.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
